title: Chapter 13: The Confession
content: <!-- Speed: Explosion | D1+D5 -->
At 5:02 p.m. Pacific, Arthur Dimmesdale's statement went live on Medium.
The title was four words: *"What I Did Was Wrong."*
The post was 3,200 words. It took Arthur seven hours to write, two hours to edit, and forty-five minutes to publish because his hands were shaking so badly he couldn't type his Medium password.
It began:

*"In March of last year, I presented a machine learning architecture to the Eden board that I claimed was my own work. It was not. It was Hester Vance's architecture. She had developed it over eighteen months as Eden's Chief Technology Officer. I had access to her research through a shared internal repository. I used that access to take her work and claim it as mine."*

It continued for 3,000 more words, documenting every step: the repository access, the presentation, the board's approval, the six weeks between the presentation and the Synapse flag that removed Hester from the platform, the months of silence while she was erased from the industry she'd built, the money he'd made, the reputation he'd stolen.
It ended:

*"I don't deserve forgiveness. I'm not asking for it. I'm asking to be held accountable for what I did, publicly and completely, so that Hester Vance receives the credit she was denied and the justice she was owed. She built something extraordinary, and I took it. The least I can do is give it back."*
By 5:14, the post had 34,000 reads.
By 5:27, it had 180,000 reads.
By 5:45, Arthur Dimmesdale was no longer the CEO of Eden.

The Eden board's statement came at 5:51 p.m. It was 127 words and contained no apology to Hester Vance. It announced Arthur's resignation — "effective immediately" — and named an interim CEO. It described the situation as "a deeply regrettable incident" and noted that the board was "conducting a thorough review of internal procedures."
It did not mention that the board had approved Arthur's architecture six weeks before Hester was flagged.
It did not mention that two board members had been involved in the Synapse flagging process.
It did not mention Hester Vance's name.
PEARL caught all three omissions in 0.7 seconds and posted them to PROJECT A's Synapse account.
The laundromat watched the confession unfold in near-silence. The team had gathered at 4:30 p.m., knowing what was coming. They'd ordered pizza. No one ate it.
"He actually did it," Zara said, reading the post on her phone. "He actually wrote it all down."
"He had a 97% chance of doing it within seven days," Keiko said. "PEARL predicted it."
"PEARL predicted it, but he still had to choose it," Hester said.

She was reading the post too, scrolling slowly, taking in every word. The architecture details were accurate. The timeline was accurate. The admission of wrongdoing was complete — no hedging, no deflection, no attempt to share blame. Arthur had written the confession the way he should have acted from the beginning: with total transparency.
The only thing missing was Roger Chillingworth's name.

Arthur hadn't mentioned Roger anywhere in the 3,200 words. He'd described his own actions — his access to the repository, his presentation to the board, his failure to credit Hester — but he'd left out the part where Roger had discovered the theft, covered it up, and weaponized it.
"He's still protecting him," Mouse said.
"He's still afraid of him," Zara corrected.
Hester didn't say anything. She just kept scrolling.
At 6:38 p.m., the Synapse Trust Score system broke.

Not metaphorically. It broke literally. Arthur Dimmesdale's confession had triggered a cascade: users who'd been flagged for similar reasons — accused of misconduct that had later been proven questionable — began posting their stories. By 6:15, #WhatIDidWasWrong was trending. By 6:30, a journalist had compiled a list of fifteen prominent figures who'd been flagged by Synapse and later exonerated — but whose Trust Scores had never been restored.

At 6:38, someone at Synapse — it was never clear who; the access logs were ambiguous — made an administrative change to the Trust Score algorithm that was intended to prevent a mass score recalculation. Instead, the change triggered a recalculation of every Trust Score in the system, dating back to launch day. The recalculation included all new data — court findings, retracted accusations, exonerations, public apologies — that had never been factored into the original scores.
The result was chaos.

A journalist who'd been flagged for "potential misinformation" had her score jump from 510 to 890 because the article she'd been flagged for had been confirmed accurate by three independent investigations.
A CEO who'd maintained a 940 Trust Score for three years dropped to 590 when the recalculation factored in an SEC settlement that Synapse's original algorithm had inexplicably ignored.
A professor who'd been flagged and silenced saw her score go from 430 to 820.
A venture capitalist with a pristine 950 dropped to 610.

By 7 p.m., Synapse had frozen all Trust Scores. An emergency notification went out: *"We are experiencing a technical issue affecting Trust Score calculations. Scores are temporarily unavailable while we investigate."*
The notification did not mention that the "technical issue" had been triggered by a confession that Synapse's own flagging system had enabled.
The notification did not mention Hester Vance.
But everyone knew.
